behaviors.description: A file placed inside a subdirectory of the watched root is processed, not only files placed directly in the root directory.
behaviors.justification: Slot A: "file placed inside a subdirectory of the watched root is processed." Slot B: real-infra (real filesystem watcher recursion and OS event delivery for nested paths). Slot C: if lowered to Integration, mocked watcher callbacks can pass even when the real watcher/glob setup never emits nested-directory events on the host OS. Rejection-gate: Agentic/Workflow are disqualified because no AI/ML model output or multi-agent sequence is involved. Final: System due to Slot B, and because Slot C is a real miss at Integration.
behaviors.test_description: Start the daemon against a real temp directory tree, create a nested subdirectory under the watched root, write a file there, and assert exactly one transformed output is produced for that nested file path.

behaviors.description: The deduplication logic correctly discards a second processing request for the same file path within a configurable time window.
behaviors.justification: Slot A: "discards a second processing request for the same file path within a configurable time window." Slot B: handoff (event/request intake -> dedup store/window check -> pipeline dispatch). Slot C: if lowered to Unit, a pure dedup function test can pass while wiring bugs still allow duplicate dispatch (e.g., path normalization mismatch between intake and dedup key). Integration-brake: (a) no required real OS/network/subprocess for this behavior itself, (b) no AI/ML output dependence, (c) no multi-step agentic chain, so no upgrade. Rejection-gate: System considered but disqualified by behavior text focusing on request-level dedup window semantics, reproducible in-process with fake clock and module wiring. Final: Integration due to Slot B, with Slot C showing Unit miss risk.
behaviors.test_description: Wire intake, dedup component, and dispatcher in-process with a controllable clock; send two requests for the same logical path within the window and assert only one dispatch call occurs, then advance beyond window and assert dispatch resumes.

behaviors.description: When the operating system emits multiple filesystem events for a single file write, the daemon processes the file exactly once.
behaviors.justification: Slot A: "When the operating system emits multiple filesystem events for a single file write." Slot B: real-infra (OS-level event burst behavior from real file writes). Slot C: if lowered to Integration, synthetic single-callback or hand-crafted duplicate events may miss timing/coalescing patterns that cause double-processing in production. Rejection-gate: Agentic/Workflow disqualified because correctness does not depend on model decisions. Final: System due to Slot B and the concrete Integration miss in Slot C.
behaviors.test_description: Run daemon against a real watched directory and perform file writes known to trigger multiple native events (e.g., write/rename or chunked write); assert one and only one output artifact/job execution for that file.

behaviors.description: If the watched directory is removed and then recreated while the daemon is running, the daemon resumes watching the new directory without a restart.
behaviors.justification: Slot A: "directory is removed and then recreated while the daemon is running" and "resumes watching ... without a restart." Slot B: real-infra (real filesystem lifecycle events and watcher re-subscription behavior). Slot C: if lowered to Integration, mocked watcher state transitions can pass while the actual OS watcher handle remains detached after inode/path replacement. Rejection-gate: Agentic/Workflow disqualified: no AI/ML output, no agent pipeline. Final: System because Slot B is decisive and Slot C is a realistic lower-level blind spot.
behaviors.test_description: Launch daemon on a real temp watched path, delete that directory, recreate it at the same path, then add a new file and verify it is processed without restarting the daemon process.

behaviors.description: When multiple files arrive at the same time, they are processed in the order determined by their creation timestamps.
behaviors.justification: Slot A: "multiple files arrive at the same time" and ordering by "creation timestamps." Slot B: real-infra (real filesystem timestamp metadata resolution/semantics and arrival concurrency). Slot C: if lowered to Integration, injected timestamps can hide platform-specific timestamp granularity/tie behavior that reorders real files. Rejection-gate: Agentic/Workflow disqualified by absence of model-dependent correctness. Final: System, driven by Slot B and the Integration miss in Slot C.
behaviors.test_description: Create multiple files nearly simultaneously in the real watched directory, capture processing order from daemon outputs/log hooks, and assert it matches filesystem creation-time ordering (with defined tie-breaker if equal).

behaviors.description: A file that is still being written to when the arrival event fires is not picked up for processing until writing is complete.
behaviors.justification: Slot A: "file that is still being written to when the arrival event fires" and delayed pickup "until writing is complete." Slot B: real-infra (real write-in-progress timing, file size/handle behavior, and watcher event timing). Slot C: if lowered to Integration, mocked 'complete write' events can pass while production still reads partial content due to race timing on real disk I/O. Rejection-gate: Agentic/Workflow disqualified because no model output is involved. Final: System due to Slot B, with Slot C showing why Integration can miss real failures.
behaviors.test_description: Write a large file in chunks with delays to the watched directory while daemon runs; assert processing does not start until final chunk/close and that output reflects full file content, not partial data.
